extra.reasoning:
The property globally(opted_out implies not sent) is an inductive invariant preserved by every transition.

Only two transitions modify the relevant variables in ways that matter:
- sent becomes true ONLY via t_send_allowed, whose guard is `not opted_out`. So whenever sent is set true, opted_out is false at that moment, and t_send_allowed does not touch opted_out. The resulting state (opted_out=false, sent=true) satisfies the implication (false antecedent).
- opted_out becomes true ONLY via t_optout, which simultaneously sets sent := false. So immediately after any optout, sent=false. There is no transition that makes opted_out true while leaving sent true.

Checking preservation on all four transitions from any state satisfying the invariant:
1. t_optout: opted_out:=true, sent:=false -> (true implies not false) holds.
2. t_send_allowed (guard not opted_out): sets sent:=true but opted_out stays false -> antecedent false, holds.
3. t_send_blocked (guard opted_out): sets sent:=false -> consequent true, holds.
4. t_tick: sets sent:=false -> consequent true, holds.

Initial state opted_out=false satisfies it vacuously. While opted_out remains true, t_send_allowed is never enabled (only t_send_blocked fires on send_attempt, and it clears sent; tick also clears sent), so sent cannot be re-set to true until an opt-in path that requires opted_out=false. Therefore in no reachable state is (opted_out=true AND sent=true) possible, within the 7-event bound or beyond. The property holds; no counterexample exists.
